definition: *Oneiromancy* is divination by dreams — the interpretation of what is seen in sleep as a message about waking events to come. Where most of the *-mancy* arts read an external medium, oneiromancy reads the diviner's own visions, treating the dream as a coded text to be decoded rather than a sign to be observed.
history: The word joins Greek *oneiros* ('dream') to *manteia* ('divination'). Its founding text is Artemidorus' second-century *Oneirocritica*, the ancient world's great dream-dictionary, whose author, scholars note, presents himself not merely as an oneiromancer but as an expert in textual practices. The art never lost its hold: Kieckhefer records that in the medieval West the interpretation of dreams remained popular, as did treatises suggesting how to interpret them. Popular and learned dream-books circulated side by side, and the Enlightenment's mockers still had to list oneiromancy among the divinations they meant to bury.

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
Artemidorus, Oneirocritica (via Edmonds et al., Magic and Religion in the Ancient Mediterranean)

etymology.note: Dream-divination, systematised in Artemidorus' 2nd-c. Oneirocritica — antiquity's dream dictionary.
